policy_statement: Provide support and incentives for municipal participation in FEMA’s Community Rating System (DEC and DHSES).
mechanism_description: Providing support and incentives for municipal participation in FEMA’s Community Rating System helps communities improve floodplain management and preparedness, reducing flood risk and increasing resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (flooding) → improved floodplain management & preparedness → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ flood impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Municipal governments adopt stronger floodplain management standards and practices (e.g., higher regulatory standards, better mapping/information, property protection measures, drainage/stormwater improvements, public awareness) encouraged by CRS participation support/incentives.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced floodwater interaction with people/assets via avoided development in high-risk areas, elevated/floodproofed structures, improved runoff conveyance/storage, and better warning/response actions.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower expected losses, service disruptions, and recovery time from climate-amplified flood events (increased resilience).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood damage and disruption by lowering exposure and vulnerability through improved floodplain management and preparedness.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on flood risk management and preparedness to reduce impacts from flooding (a climate hazard).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some CRS actions can incidentally affect emissions (e.g., rebuilding standards, land conservation), the dominant and intended effect across CRS participation is risk reduction/resilience (impact reduction), which is Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a jurisdiction uses CRS-related open-space preservation primarily for carbon sequestration, that would be a secondary nature-based co-benefit; however, the mechanism as defined is framed around flood risk reduction, so Adaptation remains primary.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy supporting/incentivizing CRS participation is fundamentally aimed at improving floodplain management to reduce flood impacts; this consistently maps to Adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., specific hazard type, nature-based elements, equity outcomes) requires policy-level evidence and is not determined at the mechanism-only level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some CRS credit activities overlap with Nature-Based Solutions (e.g., preserving floodplain open space) or Resource Efficiency (e.g., reducing repetitive loss and reconstruction), but the dominant pathway is still flood impact reduction (Adaptation) for this mechanism.
key_indicators: "provide support and incentives"; "municipal participation"; "Federal Emergency Management Agency's Community Rating System"
co_benefits: Lower NFIP insurance premiums; improved disaster preparedness/response capacity; reduced non-climate flood losses
instance_edge_case_notes: “Support” could include technical assistance (planning-strategy) as well as financial incentives; however, the explicit mention of “incentives” makes incentive-program the best single instrument type.
